insights.insight: A verified receipt eliminates the attribution problem entirely — it is the conversion event, cryptographically signed, not a modeled inference. Ad tech charges $5–$150 CPA for probabilistic attribution; this protocol settles at roughly $0.10 per verified receipt, a 50–200x cost delta.

Cookies are effectively dead across Chrome, Safari, and Firefox, and device fingerprinting is increasingly blocked — leaving brand marketers with first-party data that goes blind the moment a consumer leaves their ecosystem. Verified receipt data is identity-free and cross-ecosystem by design, which no current ad tech stack produces.

The dataset is building a rural spine across 17 CBSAs with no metro cluster anchoring it — the opposite of how ad tech data accumulates, which skews heavily toward urban device density. For brands with meaningful rural distribution (grocery, home improvement, club retail), this is a coverage gap that existing data vendors cannot fill.

King Soopers, Kroger, Walmart, Costco, and Target collectively account for the majority of verified receipts in the trailing 14-day window, with Costco averaging $101 per basket and H-E-B at $100. These are deterministic spend signals at retailers that share no purchase data with outside brands.

Amazon knows Amazon purchases, Kroger knows Kroger purchases — no retailer sees cross-chain spend, and none will share it. This protocol aggregates verified purchase signals across vendors without requiring retailer cooperation, meaning the dataset grows regardless of whether any brand or retailer participates.

insights.topic: SaaS receipt pipeline as cross-domain signal
insights.insight: Five developer infrastructure vendors — Notion, Render, Vercel, DigitalOcean, Hetzner — have open activation requests but zero receipts in the dataset today. If they activate, the protocol becomes the first verified cross-domain spend layer combining physical retail and digital SaaS commerce in a single queryable dataset.
